title: Knobs for Complexity: Underfitting vs. Overfitting
concept: How tuning hyperparameters controls model complexity, shifting it between overfitting (memorizing) and underfitting (oversimplifying).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the key difference between a parameter and a hyperparameter?

options.text: Hyperparameters are settings you choose beforehand; parameters are learned by the model.
Parameters are settings you choose beforehand; hyperparameters are learned by the model.
There is no difference; they are different names for the same thing.

exercises.explain: Correct! You set the hyperparameters (knobs) beforehand, and the model learns its parameters (weights) during training.

cards.title: The focus knob
cards.body: Imagine focusing a camera lens. If you turn the focus knob too little, the picture is a blurry smudge. If you turn it too much, it gets blurry again. You need to adjust the knob to the sweet spot in the middle to get a perfectly sharp image.

cards.title: Overfitting vs. Underfitting
cards.body:
Tuning our hyperparameter knobs controls how complex the model can be:
• Underfitting (Oversimplifying): The model is too simple to learn the trends (e.g. low tree depth, huge k).
• Overfitting (Memorizing): The model is too complex and memorizes noise (e.g. high tree depth, small k).

cards.title: Exam prep styles
cards.body:
Imagine studying for a history exam:
• Underfitting is like only reading the textbook's back cover. You learn almost nothing.
• Overfitting is like memorizing the exact font size and page numbers. You fail on new questions.
You want to learn the actual history concepts to pass!

cards.title: Tuning the knobs
cards.body:
Let's see how hyperparameters shift model complexity:
• Decision Trees: A max depth of 1 underfits (too simple). A max depth of 100 overfits (too detailed).
• kNN: A k of 100 underfits (averages everyone). A k of 1 overfits (chases single noisy points).

cards.title: Perfect training isn't perfect
cards.body:
Myth: A model that gets 100% accuracy on the training data is the best model.
Reality: 100% training accuracy usually means the model overfit by memorizing the training set. It will likely fail when it sees new data!

cards.title: Finding the sweet spot
cards.body: How hyperparameter settings affect the model's fit:

left.text:
Misses patterns.
Too simple.
Poor performance on training data and new data.

right.text:
Memorizes noise.
Too complex.
Great on training data, poor on new data.

exercises.prompt: If you set a Decision Tree's maximum depth to 100, what is the most likely outcome?

options.text: The tree will overfit by creating too many specific rules and memorizing noise.
The tree will underfit and fail to learn any patterns.
The tree will run faster because it has more branches.
The tree will automatically adjust its own depth to 5.

exercises.explain: Correct! Giving the tree unrestricted depth allows it to create highly specific rules that memorize noise (overfitting).

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a kNN model, choosing a very large 'k' (like 100) makes the model more complex and prone to overfitting.
exercises.explain: False! A very large k averages many neighbors, smoothing out predictions and making the model simpler, which can cause underfitting.

exercises.sentence: To find the perfect balance between overfitting and underfitting, we must carefully ___ our hyperparameters.
exercises.explain: Tuning is the process of adjusting hyperparameters to find the sweet spot of model complexity.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following setups is most likely to cause underfitting (oversimplification)?

options.text: A Decision Tree with a max depth of 1
A Decision Tree with a max depth of 50
A kNN model with k set to 1

exercises.explain: A Decision Tree limited to a depth of 1 can only make a single split, which is too simple to capture most patterns.

exercises.prompt: You train a kNN classifier with k=1. It gets 100% accuracy on your training data, but only 60% accuracy on new, unseen data. What should you do to improve performance on new data?

options.text: Increase k to look at more neighbors, smoothing out noise and reducing overfitting.
Keep k=1 because training accuracy is the most important score.
Decrease k to 0 to make the model even more specific.
Add duplicate data points to force the model to look at identical matches.

exercises.explain: Perfect! A k of 1 overfits by memorizing individual points. Increasing k forces the model to base predictions on an average of multiple neighbors, reducing overfitting. Next, let's learn how to systematically test different knob settings!
